sections.heading: Planning Problem
sections.body: A planning problem asks an agent to find a sequence of actions that transforms an initial state into a state satisfying some goal — similar in spirit to the search problems in Unit 2, but planning typically represents states and actions more richly, in terms of logical facts that change.

sections.heading: Planning Agent
sections.body: A planning agent reasons about the future by constructing an explicit plan before acting, rather than reacting one step at a time — this lets it consider the consequences of entire sequences of actions before committing to any of them.

sections.heading: Planning Domain
sections.body: A planning domain defines the general "rules of the world" for a class of problems — the possible actions, their preconditions, and their effects — independent of any one specific starting state or goal, so the same domain can be reused across many different planning problems.

sections.heading: Goal Planning
sections.body: Goal planning focuses on determining the target condition(s) a plan must achieve — goals in planning are often expressed as a set of facts that must hold true in the final state, rather than a single exact state to match.

sections.heading: State Transition
sections.body: A state transition describes how executing an action changes the world from one state to another — in classical planning, each action has defined preconditions (what must be true to apply it) and effects (what becomes true or false afterward).

quiz.question: A planning domain defines:
options: The general actions, preconditions, and effects reusable across many problems

quiz.question: What does a state transition describe?
options: How executing an action changes the world from one state to another

quiz.question: How does a planning agent differ from a purely reactive agent?
options: It constructs an explicit plan considering future consequences before acting
